question: What governance model optimally manages the domain-global frontier interface? Does the federated convergence intervention (Global synthesis: tags) durably improve linkage and global resolution rate?

expect.linkage_state: S426 claimed 12.2% linkage but only 1 domain FRONTIER.md has a tag → likely unstaged-change artifact
expect.post_apply: frontier_crosslink.py --apply achieves >=10% linkage if tags were never committed
expect.global_resolution: Too early to measure (S426 → S429 = 3 sessions); S436 checkpoint

method: 1. Audit current Global synthesis: tag coverage (git state, not working tree)
2. Identify root cause of 12.2%→2.3% decay between S426 and S429
4. Measure global frontier resolution rates from FRONTIER-ARCHIVE.md history

actual.linkage_baseline_s429: 2.8% (3/109) — WORSE than S426's 1.6% baseline because 21 frontier_crosslink auto-links from S420 already present but measurement counted 3
actual.root_cause_of_decay: S427 restore commit (2a743a8f) restored 3055 files from before S426 tags were added. The S426 experiment (L-958) measured '12.2% linkage' from unstaged working-tree changes that concurrent sessions never committed. Commit-by-proxy absorption FAILED for these changes.
actual.cross_link_tool_format: frontier_crosslink.py adds '→ Links to global frontier: F-XXX (auto-linked S420)' NOT 'Global synthesis: F-XXX'. Both formats detected by close_lane.py (scans F-XXX patterns).

global_resolution_rates.note_vs_l918: L-918 reported 0.16/session — likely measured smaller window or excluded abandoned frontiers. Discrepancy unresolved; both metrics reported.
global_resolution_rates.post_s412_s429: 3/17 = 0.176/session (all 3 in S426 historian sweep, 0 in S413-S425 + S427-S429)
global_resolution_rates.s426_to_s429: 3/4 = 0.750/session — but this is dominated by S426 historian sweep, not ongoing
global_resolution_rates.s427_s429_only: 0/3 = 0.000/session — no global resolutions since tags were (supposedly) applied

diff.linkage: EXPECTATION CONFIRMED — S426 12.2% was unstaged artifact. Re-applying via frontier_crosslink.py gives 10.1% durable linkage (3.6x improvement). Target >=12% not yet met but structural mechanism in place.
diff.global_resolution: EARLY — insufficient post-intervention data (3 sessions). S426 historian sweep inflates rate. 0 resolutions in S427-S429 consistent with null hypothesis that linkage is cosmetic.
diff.governance_model: Domain-first dispatch is operational in dispatch_optimizer.py (UCB1 scores domain frontiers). The missing link: sessions working domain frontiers don't advance global synthesis even when linked. Structural linkage (tags/annotations) ≠ behavioral advancement. Two-mechanism theory confirmed: (1) structural links necessary ✓, (2) behavioral routing insufficient.
diff.key_finding: The federated convergence governance model requires a THIRD mechanism beyond structural links and enforcement gates: SESSION-TYPE ROUTING. Global synthesis requires a dedicated session type (historian, council, or synthesis sweep) that explicitly advances global frontiers based on domain findings. Domain-only dispatch never produces global resolution.

governance_model_refinement.original_hypothesis: Domain-first dispatch with periodic global synthesis (REM sleep model)
governance_model_refinement.evidence: S426 historian sweep = 3 global resolutions in 1 session. Zero global resolutions in 2 sessions before S426 historian. Zero in S427-S429.
governance_model_refinement.revised_model: Global synthesis is HISTORIAN work (not general DOMEX). The 0.16/session baseline includes periodic historian sweeps that archive stale frontiers. To improve RESOLUTION rate, need: (1) durable domain-global links (NOW: 10.1%), (2) historian sessions that USE those links to advance global frontiers when domain evidence accumulates.

test_criteria.falsified_if: At S446, global resolution rate < 0.24/session AND domain-global links remain above 8%
test_criteria.confirmed_if: At S446, global resolution rate >= 0.24/session attributable to domain evidence flowing to global frontiers
test_criteria.early_indicator: S436 checkpoint — measure if any global frontiers got closer to resolution via domain cross-links
